confidence: Medium (single occurrence but 4 converging signals: S0 S0 + tana mana + llapa + 4x compensation)
summary: Murder case — the most violent khipu in the corpus. Contains the ONLY occurrence of 'mana' (negation) across all 619 khipus. The death of the mother (mama) is encoded as a 3-element periphrasis: tana(family support) + mana(no more) + llapa(divine lightning of Illapa). The uncle (kaka x3) is the accused, armed with maka (weapon x3). 9 testimony votes of guilty. Compensation of 682 units paid to the father. The khipu opens with S0 S0 (explicit double-zero) — one of only 2 such cords in the entire corpus — signaling the victim is 'counted at zero' = dead.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  89 cords, 21 clusters, 14 STRING. Museum Fur Volkerkunde, Munich.
  9-phase judicial dossier separated by empty cords.
  Contains the ONLY occurrence of "mana" (negation) in 619 khipus.

THE DEATH SIGNAL: 3 ELEMENTS
  1. S0 S0 (cord 2): explicit double-zero = the victim is counted at zero = DEAD.
     Only 2 such cords exist in the entire OKR (54,000 cords). 0.004% frequency.
  2. "tana mana" (cords 6-7): "the family support is no more" = verbal euphemism.
     The word wañuy (to die) cannot be written in ALBA (ñ is not encodable).
     The scribe uses a periphrasis instead.
  3. "llapa" with S-prefix=20 (cord 12): divine lightning of Illapa.
     Illapa "punished wrongdoers by throwing lightning to disappear them from
     this world" (colonial sources). llapa = divine judgment / death sentence.

9-PHASE READING
  Phase 1: [0, 0] + [50, 50, 30, 20]
    → S0 S0 = victim dead. Then: victim's assets (total=150).
  Phase 2: tana(support) mana(no more) maka(weapon) mama(mother) maka(weapon) chiy(?) llapa=20(divine lightning)
    → "The support is no more. Weapon against the mother. Divine judgment."
    → Then: 110, 4, 4, 3, 4 = fine amounts.
  Phase 2b: maka(weapon) kaka(uncle x3) kaki(?)
    → The accused: the uncle, who had the weapon.
  Phases 3-5: 9x figure-eight (value=1 each)
    → 9 testimony votes: GUILTY.
  Phase 6: [13, 21, 6, 5, 12] + mama(mother)
    → Compensation amounts due for the dead mother.
  Phases 7-8: [4,3,3,2,3,3] [5,3,3,2,3,5] [5,2,2,1,1,3]
    → Payment schedule details (symmetric registers).
  Phase 9: papi=10(father?) + [9,10,9,7,6,11,17,6,4,5,7] + 116(total)
    → Closure. Father receives compensation. Final total = 116.

COMPARATIVE EVIDENCE
  UR110 and UR112 have same violence+kinship pattern (maka+kaka+mama)
  but WITHOUT tana/mana/llapa/S0 → assault cases, not murder.
  Their compensation totals (169, 188) are 4x LOWER than UR1091 (682).
  The death markers are what distinguish murder from assault.

NOTE ON THE WORD "DEATH"
  The Quechua word wañuy (to die) appears to be unwritable in ALBA
  because the phoneme ñ (palatal nasal) has no known knot encoding.
  If correct, this would require the scribe to use a periphrasis such
  as "tana mana" (the support is no more) instead of a single word.
  This is a tentative observation — the reading of UR1091 as a murder
  case is one plausible interpretation among others.
narrative:
Zero. That is the first number on the khipu — not one, not ten, but zero. Two S-knots, each encoding nothing. Cord 2 carries this double absence, and in a system where every knot means something counted, a deliberate zero means something uncounted. Someone has been removed from the register. Someone is dead.

The next cords carry the dead person's assets: 50, 50, 30, 20. One hundred and fifty units of whatever she owned — herds, textiles, stored grain. The property of someone who can no longer hold it.

Then the words begin. *Tana* (cord 6) — the family support, the one who holds the household together. *Mana* (cord 7) — no more. The support is no more. In 619 khipus and 54,000 cords in the Open Khipu Repository, the word *mana* — negation, absence, not — appears exactly once. Here. On this cord. The scribe needed to say 'death,' but the Quechua word *wañuy* contains the sound *ñ*, which has no knot in the ALBA syllabary. So he wrote around it: the support is no more.

The weapon follows immediately. *Maka* (cord 8) — the war club. Then *mama* (cord 9) — mother. The weapon and the mother, side by side. *Maka* again (cord 10). And then, six cords later: *llapa* (cord 12), with a prefix value of 20. The divine lightning of Illapa, the thunder god who 'punished wrongdoers by throwing lightning to disappear them from this world.' Three elements — zero, *tana mana*, *llapa* — encode the death in three registers: numerical (counted at nothing), verbal (the support is no more), and sacred (the god has struck).

The accused is named next. *Maka* again — the weapon — and then *kaka*, *kaka*, *kaka*. The uncle, three times. He held the weapon. He is the one the scribe points to.

Nine figure-eight knots follow in phases 3 through 5, each valued at 1. Nine testimonies. Nine witnesses, or nine votes. All guilty.

Then the compensation. Phase 6 names the victim one last time — *mama*, the mother — and lists the amounts owed: 13, 21, 6, 5, 12. Phases 7 and 8 detail the payment schedule in symmetric registers, the bureaucratic machinery of Inca justice grinding through the arithmetic of a life's worth. The total reaches 682 units — four times higher than UR110's compensation of 169, four times higher than UR112's 188. The price of a death, not just an assault.

The last phase belongs to the father. *Papi* (cord 78), with value 10 — the father, who receives what is owed. Eleven more numbers follow, then a final total: 116. The case is closed. The payments are scheduled. The uncle struck the mother with a war club, and the mother is counted at zero, and the thunder god has spoken, and nine witnesses agreed, and the father will be compensated 682 units over installments that the khipukamayoq recorded on cotton thread in Munich, where it sits today.
references: Compare UR110, UR112 (assault without death markers)
